   345→**INTRODUCTION TO THE BOOK**
   346→
   347→Thank you for showing interest to read this book. With all honesty I say that I am not a born or a known author. I am a retired industry leader with 36 years of experience in various corporates. I wanted to share my views on Corporate Misgovernance, the title of this book, particularly with management students and young professionals who aspire to be the leaders of future so that they can understand various avoidable decisions taken by leaders in the past and enable them to refrain from committing similar mistakes when they assume top positions in the corporate like Senior Managers, Departmental Heads, Business Unit (BU) & Strategic Business Unit (SBU) Heads, Deputy General Managers (DGM) , General Managers (GM), Assistant Vice Presidents (AVP), Vice Presidents (VP) or the Chief Executive Officers (CXOs) , Presidents & Directors.

content.content:
350→
   351→**PREFACE**
   352→
   353→I am aware that there are thousands of books in the market on Leadership Management focusing on what Leaders should do illustrating different case studies. Though all these case studies may not be always appropriate in the Indian business environment or in the country where your organization is based, nonetheless they present very important issues handled by leaders, some of which may have had deep bearing on the viability, sustenance and profits of the organization. Leaders must learn lessons from these case studies so as to act appropriately in similar situations in future.
   354→
   355→However, this book revolves the other way round. **It focuses on what Leaders should avoid**. It cites numerous examples of wrong decisions taken by leaders, albeit in my opinion, some of which have led to serious consequences for the organization. As I grew up the ladder and eventually assumed a top position, I have come across various issues myself as well as those which I learnt from my friends who worked in different corporates at senior leadership positions. All these issues led to the compilation of this book which serves as a practical guide for young growing professionals aspiring to become leaders of future in their respective fields of expertise.
   356→
   357→Unlike many theories of management (meant for examinations?), I have narrated issues and cited decisions taken by leaders which were probably wrong in my opinion and led to various problems in the organization. In every such issue discussed, I have also tried to find out what other solution could have been more appropriate so that these problems were arrested in the first place itself.
   358→
   359→We are all human beings. Leaders too are. **“To err is human”.** People do make mistakes in life. So do leaders in organizations. Examples cited in the book are not meant to disrespect or demine any particular leader or refer to any particular individual, but rather to opine how they could have acted in a different manner. I am grateful to those same leaders belonging to various corporates from whom I have learnt so much for the growth and success in my own professional career. But, at the same time, I have analyzed how they could have taken decisions differently in many instances to avoid problems which cropped up subsequently. Some may disagree with my opinions or the solutions mentioned, nevertheless, it will provide them some food for thought on how they would like to act under such circumstances if arising in their future.
   360→
   361→The entire book has been broadly grouped into five Sections and each Section into different Parts. Each Part deals with issues of a particular domain of operations or department. Each issue of the particular domain is discussed under different chapters constituting the Part. The role of the department is first explained in the Part and thereafter each chapter against this domain discusses on a particular issue and the problems aroused and the likely solution. Some issues discussed may seem simple (not to all) and some chapters are small, may be a one pager, but I felt that such “simple or one pager” issues are worth discussing because they are important to an organization. Remember what Shakespeare said:  **“All small incidents in life together create a huge impact on our minds”.** Similarly many small decisions in the corporate may create huge impact on the organization over time. And hence the importance of recognizing issues, big or small. Some of you may already be aware of some of the issues cited and they may feel that these issues are a commonplace not worth mentioning. However, I have sometimes observed that leaders still take such inappropriate decisions and therefore these issues deserve mention and discussion for the benefit of all.
   362→
   363→Almost all the chapters of the book are in reference to construction projects in the Indian context since construction projects encompasses different domains of management. All the chapters are purposefully hilariously titled to draw your attention on the core issue. 
   364→
   365→At the end of each Part, I have also listed some good practices which may be adopted by leaders to create a **“feel good factor”,** a proper corporate governance model and a good working environment amongst employees in the organization. It’s up to you to adopt should you feel it as relevant. 
   366→
   367→In Part 26 of this book, I have explored some new concepts which come across my mind. I leave it to your wisdom to adopt them or ignore. Annexures in Part 27 of the book illustrates some concepts for your better understanding. Part 28 provide some personal advices which are interrelated between your personal and corporate life. These issues are often ignored while we are busy at work in office but they affect your life, family, work, work-life balance and your finances and most importantly your health \- mental, physical and spiritual. Hence it’s importance. Part 29 narrates some lessons I learnt in the corporate which helped me to get along with people at different challenging times.
   368→
   369→As a SBU Head, I had the privilege to work with my BU Heads, Departmental Heads of Strategy, Marketing & Business Development, Advertising, Designs & Engineering, Bidding & Costing, Project Management, Site Operations, Production Management, Supply Chain Management, Fleet and Equipment Management, Stores Management, Quality, Safety, Human Resources, Administration, Contracts & Law, Finance & Accounts, Audit, Information Technology, Learning and Development, Insurance, Ethics, ESG, CSR and learnt the varied dynamics of different domains of operations. These experiences gave me a detailed insight to taking right decisions and helped me to flag different issues discussed in the book.  
   370→   
   371→Right from beginning of my career, I had an inclination to analyze different decisions taken by leaders in various corporates under various circumstances. Such observations were of great interest to me. As I grew up with more experience, I felt that many of these decisions taken could have been different and I always wondered on what could have been done right. After I retired, I thought that the only way I can convey my thoughts on right governance to young professionals and management students was to write this book. 
   372→
   373→Small notes before you start reading the book.  
   374→Use of the words like “He/his/him” also means and includes “she/her’s/her”  
   375→Reference to People in the organization means the employees  
   376→Words like Organization, Company & Corporate are interchangeable.  
   377→Words like “you” may also refer to the organization.  
   378→Words like Project and Contract are interchangeable.  
   379→Words like Order, Award and Contract are interchangeable.  
   380→Words like Bid and Tender are interchangeable.  
   381→Words like vendor, supplier, and subcontractor are interchangeable.  
   382→Words like contractor may mean the organization itself  
   383→Words like specifications, tender or bid documents are interchangeable.  
   384→Words like client and customer are interchangeable.
   385→
   386→Last but not the least, you may mail your suggestions and feedback to [abhik.pal.tata@gmail.com](mailto:abhik.pal.tata@gmail.com) so that I too can learn from your experience and incorporate them as deemed fit in the next edition of the book. I wish all of you a great leadership in future. 
   387→
   388→**Happy reading.**
   389→
   390→**Abhik Pal**  
   391→Former Executive Vice President & SBU Head (2017-2020),   
   392→Tata Projects Limited, India  
   393→Former Head of Business Development, Marketing & Tendering (2007 \- 2017\)  
   394→Tata Projects Limited, India  
   395→**ABOUT THE AUTHOR**
   396→
   397→I am a 1984 Civil Engineering graduate from IIEST, formerly known as Bengal Engineering College, Howrah, West Bengal, a premier Engineering institute of India. I am credited with an Advanced Diploma in Management from IMT, Ghaziabad which also a premier institute of management in India.
   398→
   399→In the 36 years of my career, I had a stint of 30 years in three Tata Group companies. In the later years, I was the Head of Business Development, Marketing and Bidding for 10 years in Tata Projects and subsequently assumed the position of **Executive Vice President & SBU Head** of a specific business domain in Tata Projects for 3 years before I hanged my boots.
   400→
   401→In the initial eight years of my professional life, I was a Design/Structural Engineer involved in the designs of different kind of structures like multistoried buildings, cooling towers, high voltage transmission line towers, etc. using software tools like STAAD and SAP. I was also into hardcore software development for designs of Reinforced Concrete Structures (RCC) using Fortran 77 as the programming language. After a eight year stint in the Designs domain, I was transferred to Marketing department and started my career in the field of management. 
   402→
   403→Outside the corporate world, I am an avid lover of music, particularly instrumental music, and I play the harmonica and guitar even today, which kept me going through my bad days and times of stress while in the corporate.
   404→
   405→I was fortunate to meet and talk to Sir Ratan Tata only once in my life in 2018 during an event on “Quality” of the Tata group in Mumbai and I adored his personality, grace, humbleness, wisdom and knowledge with all his magnificent achievements at the back of my mind. This was one of the best moments of my corporate life, a moment which I would cherish for the rest of my life. 
   406→
   407→**ACKNOWLEDGEMENT**
   408→
   409→\- To my wife, Soma, having a Masters degree in Computer Science, for always encouraging me to write this new kind of book. She was witness to what I had gone through during my leadership days and had given critical suggestions from time to time. She was confident that this new kind of book will inspire youngsters to learn and act better as future leaders. Her continuous inspiration over the last two years helped me to complete writing this book.
   410→
   411→\- To my son, Sagar, who with Masters, PhD and a post Doctorate degree obtained in a reputed university of France to his credit and with an initial some years of corporate experience, have encouraged me to narrate my experiences as a senior corporate leader. He knows the value of experiences to youngsters like him which can take them to greater heights in their corporate life. He was instrumental being a critic going through the book to point out what was nicely or badly written. It helped me to modify the contents of the book wherever required and this give shape to the final book.
   412→
   413→\- To my daughter-in-law, Samta, a Management Graduate of a reputed international University in France and having significant corporate experience in management, was always inspirational to me for writing this book on issues related to management. She has always been highly inquisitive to learn on how employees can develop themselves to become great leaders and while narrating many such experiences of mine to her complex questions gave me important insights to cover some issues in the book. She too scrutinised the contents of the book which helped me to reach the final stage of the book.
   414→
   415→\- To my well-wishers and friends, who as senior leaders of different organisations provided various inputs on managerial issues which helped me to incorporate them in the book.
   416→
   417→**DISCLAIMER**
   418→
   419→I reiterate that the issues cited in the book do not refer to any particular leader and is not meant to demine or disrespect any leader. These issues as observed in different corporates including those where my friends worked as senior leaders, have been discussed only to understand the complexities and problems that wrong decisions can create and how decisions could have been taken differently, albeit again, in my opinion.
   420→
   421→The issues are important to be discussed because such issues may crop up in any organization. However, the proposed solutions to these issues which have been mentioned to have created problems in the organization may be differently opined by others; nevertheless, it provides an insight to the youngsters for evoking their thought process. That’s the objective of the book. When these youngsters would become our future leaders, their wisdom and experience will be their best judge to decide.
   422→
   423→The book is not meant for senior leaders because they must have gone through most of these issues at one point of time in their career and they have already attained maturity and wisdom to deal with them. It is meant for management students and young professionals who may be enlightened with different issues that leaders of organisations come across so that they can deal with them first hand when they become leaders in the future. 
   424→
   425→**SECTION 1 – LEADERSHIP**
   426→
   427→**PART 1**
   428→
   429→**CXO/SBU/BU/DEPARTMENT HEAD LEVEL LEADERSHIP**
   430→
   431→**The role of Leaders in a corporate**  
   432→First and foremost, remember, Leadership does not necessarily always mean a top position like the President or Director of an organization. Even the In-Charge of the Staff in a big organization is a Leader who drives all the day-to-day staff activities. The Site Manager is also a Leader under whom various positions in the project site work for the successful completion of a project. Essentially, a Leader means someone who leads his team in all the activities that the team is responsible for.
   433→
   434→However, the weightages of top leadership is much higher since his actions will have a higher and huge impact on the organization. The weightages of the departmental heads as senior leaders is definitely less than that of the Managing Director, but nonetheless it is also very important for achievement of the goals of the organization.
   435→
   436→A Leader has multifaceted role in a corporate as narrated below:
   437→
   438→1. Visionary planning – setting a clear direction for the organization in the domain he is responsible for  
   439→2. Strategic decision – evaluating the market and developing techniques which will drive to achieve his goals  
   440→3. Corporate culture – actions in line with the cultural values of the organization  
   441→4. Stakeholder relationship – building relationship with stakeholders like employees, vendors and customers  
   442→5. Risk management – identifying potential risks and developing strategies to mitigate them  
   443→6. Leadership management – develop juniors to leaders of future   
   444→7. Finance management – monitor financial health in his domain of work
   445→
   446→Let us understand the structure of an organization and its role. An organization is usually spearheaded and driven by the Board of Directors (BoD). It forms the **Vision and Mission** of the organization and layouts a structure and foundation on which the organization treads its path to meet its goals and objectives. BoD comprises of top leaders from its own company or group companies. Now-a-days it’s mandatory to include a Director from an independent company, called the Independent Director, who would evaluate the performance of the organization from a third angle perspective. A BoD also comprises of a Lady Director who can analyse a company’s performance and activities from her perspective. The BoD may have several Directors on its Board and is led by the Chairman of the company. The Managing Director is also a part of the BoD reporting to the Chairman. The Managing Director drives the organisation in line with the Vision & Mission set by the BoD.
   447→
   448→A V**ision** statement details where the organization aspires to go. Why does your company exist? What do you hope to accomplish in the next several years? These are what a Vision addresses. But a M**ission** statement defines the **organization**’s business, its objectives, and how it will reach these objectives. The entire organization must drive their actions in line with the Vision and Mission statements of their organization.
   449→
   450→The Managing Director has under him Executive Directors and various CXOs to perform vital functions of the organisation. Each Executive Director may be working with some of the CXOs. For example, the Chief Financial Officer (CFO) looks after all the financial activities of the organisation. The Chief Strategy Officer (CSO) is responsible for finalising the strategies of different businesses of the organization, and so on. Essentially, the Managing Director and below are fully active in leading all the actions of an organization. The Chairman and the BoD may not be full time active, but reviews the organizational performance and decisions from time to time.
   451→
   452→An organisation is generally structured into different **Strategic Business Units (SBUs)** catering to different businesses of the organisation. One SBU may be involved with power projects, another SBU with Infrastructure projects like the airports, townships, etc., another SBU with Chemicals, so on and so forth. Each SBU is headed by a SBU Head who reports to a CEO (one of the CXOs). Each CXO like the CEO, CSO and CFO has a team of its own and works with each Strategic Business Unit (SBU) in that business domain. Like this, there can be several CXOs in the organisation who would report to the Executive Director or the Managing Director.
   453→
   454→Each SBU may be subdivided into different **Business Units (BUs)**. For example, the Infrastructure SBU may have a BU for airports, another BU for townships, another BU for sea-ports, etc. Each BU is headed by a BU Head and reports to the SBU Head.
   455→
   456→Each SBU may have several supporting Departments to support the different services that its BUs requires for its performance. Each such Department is driven by a Head (Department Head) who works for the BUs and may have dual reporting system, reporting to the SBU Head administratively and to the concerned CXO functionally. Examples of these support departments are Human Resources (HR), Administration, Contracts & Law, Finance & Accounts (F\&A), Audit, Information Technology (IT), Learning and Development (L\&D),  Insurance, Ethics, ESG and CSR
   457→
   458→The Chairman, Managing Director, EDs, CXOs and SBU Heads usually form the Top Management level. The BU Heads and the Departmental Heads usually form the Middle Management Levels. The Managers and Officers below the Departmental Heads usually form the Junior Management level.  
   459→The **Board of Directors (BoD)** is responsible for making key strategic decisions that determine the organization’s long-term direction, governance framework, and overall oversight.  
   460→**Top Management** translates these decisions into clear strategies and actionable plans, providing leadership, direction, and accountability across the organization.  
   461→**Middle Management** ensures coordination and alignment between strategy and operations, facilitating communication, resource deployment, and smooth execution across departments and business units.  
   462→**Junior Management** carries out the day-to-day operational activities, implementing plans, delivering results, and ensuring that organizational objectives are achieved effectively and efficiently.  
   463→To summarise, The BOD decides and frames the Vision & Mission of the organisation; The Top Management provides the **Direction** guided by the Vision and Mission of the BOD: The Middle Management is responsible for the **Facilitation** to the Junior Management for seamless performance: The Junior Management is responsible for the **Implementation** of the goals of the organisation. **Decision, Direction, Facilitation and Implementation** are the  top-down process for performance in an organisation.
   464→
   465→All references and examples of leaders in the book usually refer to these Top and Middle Management Leaders whose decisions impact the organisation significantly.
   466→
   467→**A typical Organization Structure**
   468→
   469→**TOP MANAGEMENT**  
   470→|  
   471→BOD  
   472→|  
   473→Chairman  
   474→|  
   475→Managing Director  
   476→|  
   477→Executive Director / Directors  
   478→|  
   479→CXOs  
   480→|  
   481→  SBU Heads \- AVP, VP, EVP  
   482→\-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  
   483→**|**  
   484→**MIDDLE MANAGEMENT**  
   485→					|			|  
   486→BU Heads 		Departmental Heads  
   487→(DGM, GM)		(AGM, Sr. Manager)  
   488→\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  
   489→**|**  
   490→**JUNIOR MANAGEMENT**  
   491→|  
   492→Managers  
   493→|  
   494→Deputy Managers  
   495→|  
   496→Sr. Officers  
   497→|  
   498→Officers  
   499→\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

500→
   501→  			**CHAPTER : THE “TOO MANY CXO” SYNDROME**
   502→
   503→Let us see how many CXOs could possibly and theoretically fit in an organisation. The Departments the CXO may head are mentioned within brackets.
   504→
   505→CAO – Chief Administrative Officer (Administration)  
   506→CBO – Chief Business Development Officer (Business Development)  
   507→CCO – Chief Customer Officer (Business Development)  
   508→CDO – Chief Design Officer (Designs & Engineering)  
   509→CEO – Chief Executive Officer (Operations)  
   510→CFO – Chief Finance Officer (Finance)  
   511→CGO – Chief Governance Officer (Audit)  
   512→CHO – Chief Human Resources Officer (HR)  
   513→CIO – Chief Information Technology Officer (Information Technology)  
   514→CJO – Chief of Joint Operations (Operations)  
   515→CKO – Chief Knowledge Officer (Learning & Development)  
   516→CLO – Chief Learning Officer (Learning & Development)  
   517→CMO – Chief Marketing Officer (Marketing)  
   518→CNO – Chief Negotiation Officer (Supply Chain Management)  
   519→COO – Chief Operating Officer (Operations)  
   520→CPO – Chief Procurement Officer (Supply Chain Management)  
   521→CQO – Chief Quality Officer (Quality)  
   522→CRO – Chief Redresser Officer (HR)  
   523→CSO – Chief Strategy Officer (Strategy)  
   524→CTO – Chief Technology Officer (Operations)  
   525→CUO – Chief Unifying Officer (HR)  
   526→CVO – Chief Vigilance Officer (Governance)  
   527→CWO – Chief Wellness Officer (HR)  
   528→CXO – Chief eXperience Officer (Strategy)  
   529→CYO – Chief Youth Officer (Administration)  
   530→CZO – Chief Zone Officer (Operations)
   531→
   532→Some names sound funny, isn’t it? That was just to have fun and complete the series from A to Z. You can also name them as you wish\! Now let us come to the main point. 
   533→
   534→Sometimes it is observed that the Top Leader tends to recruit several CXOs thinking that it will help in different activities of the organisation seamlessly and the Top Leader can be relatively free from interventions in all the activities of the Departments. However, the problems of recruiting many CXOs are as follows:
   535→
   536→1\. The Top becomes heavy and the Employee Overheads increases, as a result of which the company becomes less competitive in the market. The Top Management must be the lightest economically in an organisation. Their total salaries and number in an organisation must be restricted to avoid a Top Heavy Organisation. Their individual salaries may be high but, just to provide an idea, for a large organization, their total salaries could be around 10% of the company’s Cost to Company (CTC) and numbers restricted to around 1% of company’s total employee strength. 
   537→
   538→The Middle Level is kept heavier and the Junior Level is kept the heaviest, both in terms of total salary and numbers. Junior Level is the implementing level, hence the heaviest. However, I have observed too many CXOs being recruited by some companies making them top heavy.
   539→
   540→2\. Too many CXOs sometimes provide their own ideas which are contradicting to each other. The leaders below CXOs get confused faced with different directions from the different CXOs. For example, a CFO instructs the international Bidding team not to bid with less than 7% profit margins anywhere abroad, but the CBO asks the same team to bid with Zero profits in a particular country as entry costs to establish as a first time entrant and subsequently increase their presence. The irony is the CFO and CBO may be reluctant to discuss the issue amongst them. 
   541→
   542→3\. Often the CXOs are egoistic and some resort to politics to prove their superiority. They try to create an atmosphere down below that their directions are the most superior and need to be followed even if not suggested by other CXOs. This creates confusion down below as to whose directions need to be followed. In the above example, the CFO might try to impose his directions on the bidding team, rather than take up the matter with the CBO with the intervention of MD and form a consensus.
   543→
   544→4\. Each CXO wants to recruit and strengthen his team in the corporate office though their concerned Departments in various SBUs may have sufficient number of people of similar expertise. For example, the Finance Department of various SBUs may already have a Chartered Accountant (CA), however the CFO may intend to recruit another CA under his belt. Also these recruits, being under CXO and stationed at corporate office, try to flex their muscles to employees of the concerned Departments. This creates a non-cooperation environment between the department and the CXO’s team.
   545→
   546→**“Too many CXO”** becomes a common chant amongst employees when they find one after another CXOs being unnecessarily recruited. 
   547→
   548→The problem is that all departments need a CXO to be monitored and directed. But how many is the question. There are simple solutions to this problem. 
   549→
   550→1. Top Leaders need to combine related functions together to reduce the number of CXO recruits. With lesser number of CXOs, there will be better unity, harmony and clarity of directions in the organisation. For example, Business Development & Marketing, Advertising, Bidding & Costing and Customer Relations can be under the CBO. Finance, Accounts and Audit can be under CFO. Administration, HR, Learning & Development, Wellness department, Unifying Officers, and Governance can be under CHRO. 
   551→
   552→2. Under each CXO, there can be different expertise in his corporate team or in the Departments to look after different activities. For example, under CHRO, there could be different Managers either in his corporate team or in the department to look after Administration, HR, Learning & Development, Wellness, and Unifying activities but CHRO remains as the overall single Chief. Also, under CFO, there could be one Chartered Accountant (CA) in his corporate team and it may not be unnecessarily duplicated by recruiting another CA in the concerned Department.  
   553→     
   554→3. Also, different CXOs should meet together to form a consensus for providing same directions to the Department or SBU Heads. For example, the CSO, CFO and the CBO must align together to decide what profit levels the Bidding team should consider to enter an international market for the first time. The profit margin to be considered could be very less if there is huge competition in that market. It could be negative, in other words, bid with anticipated loss to be sure to win, if the market is very big and the presence and learning of the market during operations is of utmost importance. It could be high if the competition is scarce and past results show that the contractors generally bid with high profit margins. There could be different scenarios, but the essence is the CSO, CBO and the CFO must form a common opinion and guide the Department in one direction. 
   555→
   556→Let me give you another example. Imagine the pandemonium that would be created if the Cricket Control Board thinks to provide best services in international cricket by recruiting a batting coach, a spin bowling coach, a fast bowling coach, a fielding coach, a wicket keeping coach, etc. Instead there can be a single coach who has a broad knowledge of all the areas of the game. In bowling itself, there are different kinds of bowlers like the off spinners, leg spinners, fast bowling, swingers, googly bowlers, etc. but that does not mean that the Board has to recruit different coaches for different kind of bowling. In batting too, some are proficient in hitting sixes, some in cover drives, and some in sweeping. All these batting abilities require different capabilities, however, irrespective of all these variations, the coach needs to be one or maximum at two who can provide all round required expertise to all the players. This would enable to provide all the players a uniform direction.
   557→
   558→Similarly pandemonium will be created if in football there are different coaches like the free kick coach, dribbling coach, penalty shootout coach, goalkeeping coach, mid fielder coach. Isn’t it?
   559→
   560→So, in an organisation, **the CXO strength must be kept to a minimum** and the various related activities of the organisation can be clubbed together under a single CXO, as illustrated above.
   561→
   562→If you become a Managing Director someday, I wish you be so, please carefully study the business model of your organization thoroughly and discuss with key people to layout your structure of CXOs. Modify them if possible if the structure does not suit with your thoughts or align with the business you are in. Provide each CXO clear roles and responsibilities having no conflict amongst each other. Organise meetings between concerned CXOs if they have different views so that a common final view can be cascaded down below to avoid confusion and provide clarity.
   563→
   564→**The salient points are:**
   565→
   566→1. Limit your number of CXO recruits  
   567→2. Limit CXO salaries as a maximum percentage of company salary  
   568→3. Engage relevant CXOs to form a consensus on strategic decisions  
   569→4. Eliminate redundancy in positions amongst Departments & CXO’s office.
   570→
   571→**CHAPTER : THE “RENOWNED GUY” SYNDROME**
   572→
   573→Right person for the right job\! Readers may think what new I am saying. Yes, nothing new. Yet I cannot but state its importance again and again because I have seen organisations faltering in this area.
   574→
   575→I have observed that often very renowned personnel are recruited for important positions but eventually they have been found to be non-performers. Why? Simply because the recruit is not the expertise in the particular field even if he is renowned by name. You have to question what are you actually renowned for?
   576→
   577→For example, with due respect, Sachin Tendulkar was one of the renowned cricket players of the world but he would have probably performed badly if he was made the Hockey captain of India. Similarly Pele or Maradona were renowned footballers on earth, but they were unlikely to have performed as the Cricket captain of Brazil or Argentina. Am I correct? A Gynecologist and a Cardiologist both may have a MBBS degree and may be renowned, but you cannot expect a Gynecologist to perform a Heart Operation. Such blunders do happen in the corporate. Someone in the industry may be very renowned in Marketing but he cannot perform well if he is put into Operations. Sometimes HR gets swayed away on a person being so renowned, but the question is whether he is the right person for the right job that you are looking for?
   578→
   579→Mistakes are committed in another aspect also. Suppose you interview a Procurement officer working in a small sized SBU of a large sized company. The particular SBU may be dealing with few vendors on a small scale of annual procurements. But you get swayed away by the fact that the Procurement officer works in a very large company and thereafter provide him the position of Chief Procurement Officer in your company where you need to deal with hundreds of vendors on a large scale of annual procurements. Can he perform? Definitely not. It’s like asking the pilot of a small propeller jet to fly an A380 Airbus. Imagine the likely fate of the passengers\!
   580→
   581→Recruitment in senior positions must consider the following:
   582→
   583→1\. Understand from various sources about a new recruit on his field of expertise, the volume he deals with and the number of employees who report to him. Many candidates fake experience. A Project Manager may cite Bidding experience to try out for a senior post in Marketing. The HR must verify the credentials and actual experience. The scale of operations handled by the candidate is also to be determined. Customers and vendors may be consulted to get ideas about the candidate. Do not get swayed away just because he may be renowned in the industry, or he may be someone belonging to a large organization of repute but actually handling small scale operations.
   584→
   585→2\. HR needs to be careful about the qualifications versus experience issue. I will not prefer a highly qualified person having average skills and experience in small sized corporates; rather I would recruit a moderately qualified person having much greater experience in bigger corporates. Qualifications do count a lot. The IIT, IIM and other reputed institutional brands do count, but not at the cost of experience. I have great respect for well qualified professionals, but I have found many mediocre qualified employees performing better than them, sheerly because of better and wider experience. You may not know that most Directors of different corporates were not the best qualified engineers with the highest marks. They were definitely reasonably well qualified but with great acumen and experience to lead organisations. You may have scored the highest in your Law degree course, but you may not necessarily be the best Lawyer on earth\! Therefore while HR must consider qualifications for recruits, gauging their experience in the particular field is also extremely important.
   586→
   587→3\. Another critical point. Experience does not necessarily mean the number of years of experience after your college degrees; it is more about the talent and capability you have earned to deal with critical issues during your experience. There are middle level professionals who can deal with critical problems in a far better manner than a senior professional. Well, the question then comes, how did he at all become a senior professional? The answer could be two fold. In the earlier days, seniority was achieved by the number of years you spent after your college degrees and you may have risen based on total years of experience or you may have risen through unethical support by your unethical and biased Leaders. The point is, count on the talent and potential of the candidate rather than concentrating on his total experience. 
   588→
   589→You will find a chapter dedicated to this later titled “Grow by KNOWLEDGE, not by AGE”. Today, corporates hunt talent; they do not necessarily hunt seniority.
   590→
   591→**The salient points are:**
   592→
   593→1. Recruit right expertise for the right job, not look at qualifications alone  
   594→2. Renown does not find a place, the right expertise has  
   595→3. Total experience does not count, what counts is the talent, capability and potential  
   596→
   597→**CHAPTER : THE “OIL CHAIN” SYNDROME**
   598→
   599→Senior Leaders must recruit other junior leaders on their merit, talent, potential and credentials. Same should be the principle while evaluating existing leaders. But sometimes senior leaders fall prey to their juniors who have expertise to find ways to make their bosses happy (this process is called oiling) through unethical means.
   600→
   601→What is oiling? Hahaha\! Everyone knows about it. But still let me try to explain it in context to corporates. Any unethical means to keep one’s boss or seniors happy is called oiling. The irony is some bosses like it. Oiling may include saying “yes sir” to everything that the boss says even if it is not right, always tells boss how great a leader he is though in reality he is not, ignores his duty and assists the boss in his personal works to please him, doing some household chores for the boss’s family, gifting the boss or his family expensive gifts, arranging parties for boss/family at his own cost, so on and so forth.
   602→
   603→An “oil chain” means a chain of leaders oiling up the ladder. Say a Department Head oils his boss who is the Business Unit Head, the Business Unit Head oils his boss who is the SBU Head, the SBU Head oils the COO, the COO oils the ED and the ED oils the MD and so on. This is an example of “Oil Chain”. The chain can be long or short depending on the chain of personalities involved. If a leader is straight forward, honest, ethical and unbiased, the oil chain stops at his subordinate level. However, this subordinate may oil the boss of his boss so that his boss gets eliminated in the chain.
   604→
   605→People are involved in the oil chain mainly to seek undue favours like promotion for himself or someone else, increments, bonus, favours in work sphere, relief from punishments in case of an offence, undue facilities like international travel, office car use, etc.  
   606→
   607→Resorting to oiling by these elements in the organization creates mistrust amongst honest employees who toil hard for the organization; they feel cheated. These honest employees may even seek to resign and try for better prospects in other good organisations. So an organisation may lose some of their talent in the process. It is said that most, may be 80%, leave organisations because of their bosses, not because of the organization itself. Therefore a boss needs to be honest and unbiased and refrain himself from being oiled.  
   608→
   609→Every leader is recruited by his higher ups at some point or the other. The HR or the senior leader in charge of such recruitments must be careful to check the **personality and sincerity of the individual**, other than his qualifications, experience, knowledge, skills and talent. Right and honest personalities keep an organisation moving from strength to strength ensuring harmony and equality amongst people. An organisation cannot afford to lose honest and talented individuals just because of their unethical bosses. Dishonest, oiling and oiled leaders cannot take an organization to great heights.
   610→
   611→Once I had an interesting discussion with one of my Executive Directors as to why many government and private organisations look into performance of individuals in Classes X and XII. What do such results mean in a corporate? The answer was very interesting.
   612→
   613→It is said that a person attains his personality traits by the age of 18\. People may be able to change their personalities thereafter but only to a certain small extent. The bulk of the characteristics of a human being are attained normally by 18 years of age. Therefore good marks scored in Classes X and XII indicate the past sincerity of the individual. If his results were good in Class X (aged approx. 16 at the time) and in class XII (aged approx. 18 at the time), it is commonly assumed that the person is sincere even now. This may not be true at all times; there are insincere people today who were sincere in their early lives and also vice-versa. However, looking into school and college results may give some idea about the sincerity of people. And it is assumed by an organisation that such sincere students would continue to work sincerely in the corporates.
   614→
   615→The moot point here is that the importance of one’s personality and sincerity of an individual cannot be over emphasised. The HR Head must observe or get feedback from deprived employees of any biased decisions taken by their leaders in the appraisal process. Oil chains significantly disturbs the appraisal process, which means, often the right candidates are not rightly rewarded. If required for senior leaders, HR can take a 360 degree feedback from those who report to the seniors with regard to their performance and appraisals. Oil chains must be eliminated to ensure that good talent prosper in the organization. Let us summarise the possible solutions;
   616→
   617→1. HR must recruit or leaders must appraise based on merit (qualifications and experience), talent, potential and credentials of employees ignoring the oil chain, if any.  
   618→     
   619→2. HR must try to ascertain the personality and character of an individual so that oil chains do not flourish.  
   620→3. HR may keep a good watch during appraisal process where the oiled leaders tend to give good appraisals to underserving employees.
   621→
   622→4. HR must also note the achievement of individuals from time to time so that they can also contribute positively during the appraisal process.  
   623→     
   624→
   625→**The salient points are:**
   626→
   627→1. Appraise based on merit, talent, potential and credentials  
   628→2. Discourage oiling and break oil chains  
   629→3. Personality, honesty and sincerity of an individual matters, not only his qualifications, experience, knowledge, skills and talent  
   630→4. If bosses display true unbiased leadership, talent can be retained
   631→
   632→**CHAPTER : THE “LETHARGIC AND REACTIVE” SYNDROME**
   633→
   634→Have you read the book “The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People”. The first chapter itself deals with the habit of being proactive. 
   635→
   636→Please understand that some of your competitors, may not be all, are always on the lookout for opportunities. So when an organisation comes across an opportunity, it should be quick to act upon. Or if the CSO has come up with a good Strategy, the organization must be quick to implement the same. 
   637→
   638→Once at a point of time, an organization had ample opportunity to bag construction projects in an African country. But there were lot to study and understand to avail the opportunities like the labour and tax laws of the country, the infrastructure available, the anticipated profit levels, the vendors and subcontractors available, the award system, the repatriation laws, the organization’s manpower required in the country, the office location, overall costs, etc.
   639→
   640→Every analysis for the above started but very slowly. It was more than a year that above analysis was completed. By the time, one competitor did their due diligence fast and was quick to secure an order too. This organization was late. When an organization enters a new market for the first time with the presence of only a few competitors, you can expect good profit margins. But as time goes by, more competitors come in, competition increases, chances to win decreases, and profit margins dwindles even if you win. 
   641→
   642→In the life time of a particular business, whether the business is related to a product or a service or a project where products and services are integrated, you will find that the profit margins during the early period of the lifetime is high and thereafter it diminishes to a point when running the business makes no sense. That is why you need to attempt and grab opportunities at the earliest. If the opportunities require lot of due diligence before you dive into those, you need to study and complete your due diligence and take necessary actions very fast so that you can thereafter try to avail of those opportunities. The sooner, the better, is the mantra. 
   643→
   644→Also an organization must be innovative to produce new products or a service or find new methods and new technologies to implement a project. However, even a new product or service will have a lifetime with the phenomenon of diminishing returns over a period of time. So the constant endeavor is to constantly change with constantly high speed. It could be a change in the market, change in technology, change in the product or service, etc. Constant change and upgrading an organization is the key to success but such changes have to be fast in order to grab the opportunities with optimum profits.
   645→
   646→In another example, an organization had lot of business opportunities in some countries. But the problem was the prices to be quoted were fixed. The projects involved many raw materials for supplies where there were fluctuations in the cost of raw materials over time. Also the bid prices to be quoted were in dollars whereas the supporting vendors quoting to the organization were in INR (Indian Rupees). So there was currency fluctuation risk too involved in the bid price. To be safe from such fluctuations, the organization knew that there were hedging processes available in banks which charged a premium for the coverage. But the organization did not know the entire process of hedging and the cost thereof. Studies started but at a very slow pace. During this time, the competitors, who were already experienced in the hedging process, grabbed lot of opportunities with good profits.
   647→
   648→Therefore the faster you grab an opportunity, the better returns and also the number of opportunities you get. You must be proactive all the time. That’s the essence. Don’t be lethargic and reactive and then go to a saturated market after your competitors have grabbed the initial opportunities. The CSO, CBO and the SBU Head must act very fast and request other CXO or Department Heads to be proactive to learn the required new aspects of an opportunistic market and dive into the market as soon as possible to reap the maximum benefits. 
   649→
